Customize Windows Terminal with oh-my-posh (PowerShell Prompt + Windows Terminal Setup)

Staring at the same plain PowerShell prompt all day gets old. This post walks through giving Windows PowerShell a git-style prompt with oh-my-posh, plus installing and customizing Windows Terminal (the newer Microsoft terminal) — themes, fonts, background image. Heads up — this article was written in 2019; oh-my-posh has since moved to v3+ which uses different config commands.

Whenever you submit a form, there's usually some kind of validation kicking in behind the scenes. So what about ThinkPHP? It turns out ThinkPHP ships with its own form validation feature, so in this post I'll walk through how to use ThinkPHP's built-in validation mechanism.
RE: Learning JS From Zero — Day 9: Logical Operators (and Why They Don't Always Return true/false)
RE：從零開始的學習 JS 生活
第 11 屆 iT邦幫忙鐵人賽

Day 9 of getting reincarnated into JS land. Back on Day 6 we covered operators, but we skipped the logical operators — &&, ||, and !. So this time we'll fill that gap, walk through what each one does, and end with a neat catch from Kuro: these operators don't always hand you a clean true or false.
JS From Zero EP.8 — Anonymous Functions and IIFEs

Day 8 of getting reincarnated into JavaScript. Last time we covered functions, so this time we're looking at anonymous functions — functions with no name. We'll also dig into IIFEs (immediately invoked function expressions), why they don't pollute the global scope, and the classic loop-with-setTimeout gotcha.
ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 22 - Comparison & Conditional Tags

This post covers another batch of built-in tags: comparison and conditional tags. You'll run into these a lot in real ThinkPHP projects, so I'll walk through eq, neq, the greater-than / less-than family, and then the more familiar if and switch tags. By the end you'll know which one to reach for and why both styles still show up in legacy code.
ThinkPHP Built-in Tags (1): Looping with volist, for, and foreach

We've actually been using a few of ThinkPHP's built-in template tags in earlier chapters, like volist, but I never stopped to explain what they actually do. So this kicks off a small series on the built-in tags. First up: the three looping tags. We'll cover volist (the powerful one), the simple for loop, and foreach — including the parameters like offset, length, empty, and key that make volist so handy.
Re:Learning JS From Zero — Day 7: Functions, Your Reusable Code Wrappers

Day 7 of my JS isekai. This time we're getting into functions — one of those things you absolutely have to understand to write code. We'll look at why functions exist, how to define a named function, what that mysterious event parameter is, and how wrapping repetitive code saves you from editing the same thing 100 times.
RE:Zero — Learning JS From Scratch, Day 6: Operators (and Why 1 + '1' Is '11')

Day 6 of my reincarnated JS life. This time we cover operators. We've already bumped into a few of them, but here I want to walk through the common ones properly — arithmetic operators, comparison operators, loose vs strict equality, and that weird thing where 1 + '1' gives you '11' instead of 2.
Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 20 - Pulling Bootstrap into Your Layout

It's finally time to bring in some third-party resources. In this post I'll pair Bootstrap with the Layout we built last time, so you can clearly see the difference between a layout that loaded correctly and one that didn't. I'll walk through where to drop the files, how to link them, and the shorthand tags ThinkPHP gives you for loading CSS and JS.
ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 19: Building Layouts with the Built-in Template Engine

ThinkPHP ships with its own HTML template engine, so this time we'll look at how to build a Layout with it. We'll split a page into header and footer, pull them in with the include syntax, and then level up to a proper layout file with a CONTENT placeholder. By the end you'll see that the built-in template engine is honestly good enough for most projects.
